name: Wolfe and Barber Human-scored Simulated Essay Agreement Extract
population_or_context: Automated essay scoring data augmentation using LLM-simulated essays generated from real source essays.
sample_size: 96 real student essays and 288 simulated essays generated from three prompt-engineering strategies.
methods: Extracted the expert scoring, score preservation, agreement metrics, weighted kappa, score distribution, and realism-judgment evaluation for simulated essays.
education_context: Student essay responses scored on a four-point rubric.
response_origin: Simulated essays are generated from real student essays and evaluated against the original source essay scores and expert judgments.
label_source: Original real essays had professional ratings; expert raters scored and judged the simulated essays for score preservation and realism.
generation_model: Large language model; the public preprint text reviewed here does not name a specific model version.
generation_method: Prompt-based simulation from real source essays using three strategies.
prompt_strategy: 25 examples, Predict Next, and Sentence prompting strategies are compared.
evaluation_method: Human scoring agreement between original and simulated essays, weighted kappa, exact and adjacent agreement, score-distribution comparison, and expert realism ratings.
scorer_use: Generated essay calibration and data-augmentation suitability for automated scoring research.
real_response_comparator: Each simulated essay is tied to a real source essay whose score supports score-preservation comparison.
reported_metrics: Exact agreement, adjacent agreement, weighted kappa, average-score differences, score-distribution results, and realism ratings.
limitations: Real source essays condition the generated essays, so comparator independence is limited.
Human score preservation and realism do not establish downstream automated-scorer validity without separate scorer validation.
